quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Photoelectric effect"?

options.text: v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself
Total A (mass number), total Z (charge), total energy (including mc²), and total momentum are all conserved
Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT
Light ejects electrons only if hf ≥ φ (threshold frequency); KE_max = hf - φ; explained by photon model, not wave model

quiz_questions.explanation: "Photoelectric effect" is defined as: Light ejects electrons only if hf ≥ φ (threshold frequency); KE_max = hf - φ; explained by photon model, not wave model. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Photon"?

options.text: Path difference = 2nt (n = film index, t = thickness); phase shifts at boundaries with higher-n materials; produces colored patterns
When a driving frequency matches a natural frequency of the tube, standing waves form and the sound is amplified dramatically
F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B
A quantum of electromagnetic radiation; energy E = hf; momentum p = h/λ = hf/c; no rest mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Photon" is defined as: A quantum of electromagnetic radiation; energy E = hf; momentum p = h/λ = hf/c; no rest mass. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Work function (φ)"?

options.text: Net E at a point = vector sum of fields from all individual charges; requires component-wise addition
Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
Point fingers in direction of v (or I), curl toward B; thumb points in force direction for positive charge
The minimum energy needed to eject an electron from a material's surface; material-dependent; determines threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h

quiz_questions.explanation: "Work function (φ)" is defined as: The minimum energy needed to eject an electron from a material's surface; material-dependent; determines threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "De Broglie wavelength"?

options.text: Weak attraction to B fields; atoms have permanent magnetic dipoles that partially align with the field; effect disappears when field is removed
V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier
λ = h/p = h/(mv); all particles have wave-like properties; significant only for very small masses (electrons, atoms, not baseballs)

quiz_questions.explanation: "De Broglie wavelength" is defined as: λ = h/p = h/(mv); all particles have wave-like properties; significant only for very small masses (electrons, atoms, not baseballs). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Photons and Quantum Mechanics, which best defines "Wave-particle duality"?

options.text: Particles exhibit wave behavior (diffraction, interference) and waves exhibit particle behavior (photoelectric effect); fundamental principle of quantum mechanics
f_observed = f_source · (v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source); approaching gives higher pitch, receding gives lower pitch
Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT
Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows

quiz_questions.explanation: In Photons and Quantum Mechanics, "Wave-particle duality" refers to: Particles exhibit wave behavior (diffraction, interference) and waves exhibit particle behavior (photoelectric effect); fundamental principle of quantum mechanics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Light ejects electrons only if hf ≥ φ (threshold frequency); KE_max = hf - φ; explained by photon model, not wave model"?

options.text: RMS values
Total internal reflection
Photoelectric effect
Entropy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Photoelectric effect".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A quantum of electromagnetic radiation; energy E = hf; momentum p = h/λ = hf/c; no rest mass"?

options.text: Photon emission
Photon
Charge conservation
Nuclear fusion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Photon".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The minimum energy needed to eject an electron from a material's surface; material-dependent; determines threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h"?

options.text: Work function (φ)
Kirchhoff's loop rule
Absorption spectrum
Multi-lens systems

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Work function (φ)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "λ = h/p = h/(mv); all particles have wave-like properties; significant only for very small masses (electrons, atoms, not baseballs)"?

options.text: De Broglie wavelength
Convex mirror
Combined power of thin lenses in contact
Bohr model of hydrogen

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "De Broglie wavelength".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Photons and Quantum Mechanics, which term matches: "Particles exhibit wave behavior (diffraction, interference) and waves exhibit particle behavior (photoelectric effect); fundamental principle of..."?

options.text: Wave-particle duality
Malus's law
Kinetic theory
Thin-film interference

quiz_questions.explanation: In Photons and Quantum Mechanics, this refers to "Wave-particle duality".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Photons and Quantum Mechanics is accurate?

options.text: Photons and Quantum Mechanics introduces the quantized nature of light and energy.
The strong nuclear force binds nucleons together, overcoming electrostatic repulsion between protons.
This force is short-range (~1 fm), extremely strong, and charge-independent.
The resolution limit of optical instruments is set by the Rayleigh criterion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Photons and Quantum Mechanics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Photons and Quantum Mechanics?

options.text: The intensity and spectrum of thermal radiation depend on temperature (Wien's law: λ_max = b/T; Stefan-Boltzmann: P = σAT⁴).
Planck proposed that energy is emitted/absorbed in discrete quanta: E = nhf.
Converging (convex) lenses are thicker in the middle and bring parallel rays to a focus; diverging (concave) lenses are thinner in the middle and spread rays apart.
This force is short-range (~1 fm), extremely strong, and charge-independent.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Photons and Quantum Mechanics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Photons and Quantum Mechanics?

options.text: Energy and Mass Equivalence is summarized by Einstein's famous equation E = mc².
Einstein explained the photoelectric effect: photons with E = hf eject electrons only if hf ≥ φ (work function); the maximum kinetic energy of ejected electrons is KE_max = hf - φ.
The law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection (θᵢ = θᵣ), measured from the normal.
The force on a current loop creates torque: τ = nIAB sin θ, the basis for electric motors.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Photons and Quantum Mechanics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Photons and Quantum Mechanics?

options.text: The second law states that natural processes increase the total entropy of the universe; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse, without external work.
Sound intensity follows an inverse-square law: I ∝ 1/r².
The photon model explains why increasing intensity (more photons) increases current but not electron energy, while increasing frequency increases individual electron energy.
Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Photons and Quantum Mechanics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Photons and Quantum Mechanics?

options.text: EMF can be induced by changing B, changing A, or changing the angle θ between B and the area vector.
Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.
Power dissipated: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.
De Broglie extended wave-particle duality: all matter has a wavelength λ = h/p.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Photons and Quantum Mechanics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Photons and Quantum Mechanics?

options.text: CD/DVD reading: a laser reflects off pits and lands with different path lengths, creating constructive and destructive interference that encodes digital data
Solar cells convert photon energy to electrical energy using the photoelectric effect: photons above the bandgap energy create electron-hole pairs that generate current
Nuclear weapons: a 20-kiloton bomb converts only about 1 gram of matter to energy (E = mc² = 0.001 kg × 9 × 10¹⁶ = 9 × 10¹³ J ≈ 20 kilotons of TNT)
CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Photons and Quantum Mechanics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Photons and Quantum Mechanics?

options.text: Electron diffraction microscopy: electrons with de Broglie wavelength ~0.01 nm reveal atomic-scale structures in crystals—confirming matter's wave nature
Doppler radar: police speed guns and weather radar measure the frequency shift of reflected radio waves to determine object velocity
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
Scuba diving: at depth, the increased pressure compresses air in the lungs (Boyle's law); divers must ascend slowly to avoid decompression sickness (the bends)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Photons and Quantum Mechanics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Photons and Quantum Mechanics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Night vision devices use the photoelectric effect: photons from dim scenes eject electrons from a photocathode, which are then amplified to produce a visible image
Radio reception: AM radio waves (λ ≈ 200-600m) diffract around buildings and mountains, while FM (λ ≈ 3m) requires more line-of-sight; why AM reaches farther in hilly terrain
Airplane wing lift: the curved upper surface forces air to move faster (lower pressure above) than the flat lower surface (higher pressure below), creating net upward force via Bernoulli's principle
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Photons and Quantum Mechanics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Photons and Quantum Mechanics?

options.text: Solar cells convert photon energy to electrical energy using the photoelectric effect: photons above the bandgap energy create electron-hole pairs that generate current
PET-CT combines metabolic imaging (PET using ¹⁸F-FDG) with anatomical imaging (CT X-rays) for precise cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring
Musical instruments: standing waves on strings and in air columns produce specific frequencies (harmonics); the length, tension, and density determine the pitch
Gas identification: each gas produces unique spectral lines—used to identify unknown gases in chemistry labs, environmental monitoring, and forensics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Photons and Quantum Mechanics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Photons and Quantum Mechanics in practice?

options.text: Airplane wing lift: the curved upper surface forces air to move faster (lower pressure above) than the flat lower surface (higher pressure below), creating net upward force via Bernoulli's principle
Electron diffraction microscopy: electrons with de Broglie wavelength ~0.01 nm reveal atomic-scale structures in crystals—confirming matter's wave nature
Quantum computing chips use superconducting circuits (transmon qubits) cooled to 15 millikelvin—colder than outer space—to maintain quantum coherence for computation
Scanning tunneling microscopes use quantum tunneling current between a sharp tip and a surface to image individual atoms on surfaces with sub-atomic resolution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Photons and Quantum Mechanics.
